talk_metadata.institution_or_pharma: University of Pittsburgh and UPMC Hillman Cancer Center
talk_metadata.talk_title: Consumer Wearable Devices for Physical Functioning, Readmission Risk, Falls, and Survival in Cancer Patients
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we didn't see this rate decline over time"
Speaker said: "we found that our older patients were better at collecting Fitbit data than our younger patients"
Speaker said: "we saw a striking race difference with our non‑white participants"
Speaker said: "I'm not suggesting that this is causal. There's a lot of other factors that could be at play"

key_data_gaps: No overall survival data presented for the chemotherapy cohort beyond median follow‑up
No external validation of gait cadence and activity fragmentation thresholds
Limited racial/ethnic diversity – only 17% non‑white participants

crispro_opportunity.description: Peak gait cadence and activity fragmentation predict survival independent of ECOG and step count, suggesting a novel prognostic biomarker that CrisPRO could model and validate across larger cohorts.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "only peak gait cadence and activity fragmentation remained significant after adjusting for age, cancer stage, type, ECOG, and step count"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate continuous wearable metrics with clinical covariates to develop a composite prognostic score and test its predictive power in multi‑institutional datasets.

crispro_opportunity.description: Low activity fragmentation is associated with higher mortality, indicating a possible physiologic resistance pathway (frailty) that could be targeted with interventions.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "patients that had more fragmented activity in the yellow had greater mortality over the follow‑up"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO’s AI can identify patients with high fragmentation and recommend tailored exercise or supportive care programs to mitigate frailty‑related resistance.

crispro_opportunity.description: Future trials could stratify enrollment by baseline gait cadence to avoid signal dilution from low‑risk patients.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "these gait cadence and activity fragmentation measures might be capturing something independent of what clinicians can perceive"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate trial power under different stratification schemes using existing wearable datasets to optimize design.

crispro_opportunity.description: The retrospective analysis shows that wearable‑derived metrics have prognostic value even when traditional clinical endpoints are modest; CrisPRO could repurpose existing wearable data to rescue underperforming biomarkers.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "we found that step count did predict survival, but peak gait cadence and activity fragmentation were stronger"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Apply CrisPRO’s multimodal modeling to re‑analyze legacy trial data with wearable streams, potentially revealing hidden efficacy signals.

cited_competitors.name: Fitbit
cited_competitors.context: primary device used for data collection; discussed advantages and limitations

cited_competitors.name: Apple Watch
cited_competitors.context: mentioned as alternative but less platform‑agnostic

cited_competitors.name: Garmin
cited_competitors.context: raised by audience as alternative device

unresolved_questions: How early can gait cadence or activity fragmentation predict readmission or mortality in a prospective, real‑time setting?
What interventions can effectively modify low gait cadence or high activity fragmentation to improve outcomes?

companies_to_monitor: UPMC Hillman Cancer Center wearable program

talk_metadata.talk_title: Digital Trials and AI‑Enabled Wearable Monitoring Across Diseases
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we were able to launch the study at the end of March of 2020, just a few weeks after the pandemic started"
Speaker said: "the alarm, since it has a cost also for the participant, we try to trigger it only if we are pretty sure that there is something consistent happening"
Speaker said: "we don't want to return medical answers to these people. We want to invite them to a study"

trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: Early detection of infectious disease via resting heart rate and activity changes
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: All‑comer remote enrollment without pre‑screening for baseline health status may dilute signal in asymptomatic participants
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: No concurrent virologic testing at enrollment to confirm infection status
trial_dilution_risk.static_vs_dynamic_failure: Initial design relied on a single‑day alert threshold; later refined to require multi‑day consistent changes
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: High false‑positive rate observed when alerts were sent based on single‑day deviations

competitive_moat_weakness.vulnerable_ip_or_chemistry: Open‑source data aggregation platforms for multi‑device wearables
competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker noted reliance on third‑party aggregators that are expensive and not proprietary, suggesting limited IP protection
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Broad, disease‑agnostic enrollment may limit ability to demonstrate disease‑specific efficacy for oncology applications

key_data_gaps: Limited disease‑specific validation for oncology patients
No real‑time intervention efficacy demonstrated in the infectious‑disease alert trial

crispro_opportunity.description: CrisPRO can provide a unified, oncology‑focused analytics layer on top of heterogeneous wearable streams, addressing the current reliance on generic open‑source pipelines.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "there are a number of companies out there that are data aggregators, ... they are kind of expensive"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Deploy CrisPRO’s proprietary modeling to harmonize data across Fitbit, Apple Watch, Garmin, and Oura without costly third‑party aggregators.

crispro_opportunity.description: Incorporate multi‑day physiological change criteria and disease‑specific thresholds (e.g., for immunotherapy‑related toxicity) into digital trial protocols.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "we usually waited at least a couple of days, and we look at changes in resting heart rate and in at least one between sleep and physical activity before triggering an alarm"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate different alert windows and optimize sensitivity/specificity for oncology endpoints such as cytokine release syndrome.

crispro_opportunity.description: Partner with oncology groups to embed CrisPRO analytics into remote monitoring of checkpoint inhibitor trials, leveraging the foundation model concept.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "the idea is really to study the individual, ... and then trigger changes with respect to this normal"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Use CrisPRO’s foundation model fine‑tuned on oncology patients to predict irAE risk from wearable data.

cited_competitors.name: Fitbit
cited_competitors.context: primary device used in many of the described studies

cited_competitors.name: Garmin
cited_competitors.context: cited by audience as alternative device

unresolved_questions: What is the optimal multi‑day window for triggering infectious‑disease alerts without causing alert fatigue?
How can wearable‑derived early signals be linked to specific oncology toxicities (e.g., immune‑related adverse events)?

assets_to_track: Scripps Digital Trial Center remote monitoring platform

talk_metadata.talk_title: AI Integration of Wearable Data into Oncology Clinical Practice
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "when you look at the meta‑analysis, cancer patients are not super active in general"
Speaker said: "physicians are only right 40% of the time when estimating performance status"
Speaker said: "the survival difference from mis‑estimated performance status is about the same benefit as cisplatin in the adjuvant setting"
Speaker said: "remote patient monitoring code was billed for less than 1% of Medicare cancer patients"

vulnerability_identified.failing_compound_or_target: Remote patient monitoring (RPM) implementation in oncology
vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Lack of integration with existing EMR workflows and clinician alert fatigue
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: Speaker said: "clinicians get alert fatigue and really oftentimes don't want to hear what's happening to every one of their patients"

trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: Patient‑reported outcomes improving survival via remote monitoring
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: Broad inclusion of all lung cancer patients without stratifying by digital literacy or device ownership
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: Objective wearable‑derived activity or physiologic metrics to complement PROs
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Underpowered to detect survival benefit after initial positive signal

competitive_moat_weakness.vulnerable_ip_or_chemistry: Proprietary AI models for performance‑status estimation from Kinect video
competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed that physician scoring variability is high, indicating the model may not yet be robust enough for clinical deployment
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Reliance on specialized hardware (Kinect) limits scalability across health systems

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that physicians correctly estimate performance status only 40% of the time, yet concluded that "remote patient monitoring improves survival" without presenting prospective interventional data

key_data_gaps: No randomized controlled trial demonstrating survival benefit of RPM in oncology
Limited data on long‑term adherence to wearables in cancer cohorts
Absence of cost‑effectiveness analysis for health‑system adoption

crispro_opportunity.description: Integrate CrisPRO’s multimodal AI platform with existing RPM workflows to provide actionable risk scores (e.g., early decompensation, irAE prediction) that address clinician alert fatigue.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "we need a layer of artificial intelligence in order to make these continuous results interpretable"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can aggregate wearable, PRO, and EMR data into a single risk dashboard with configurable alert thresholds.

crispro_opportunity.description: Design oncology RPM trials that stratify patients by digital literacy and baseline activity, and include objective wearable endpoints alongside PROs.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "most patients aren't health literate, can't really do digital health things"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Use CrisPRO simulations to estimate power under different stratification schemes before launching the trial.

crispro_opportunity.description: Demonstrate that AI‑derived performance‑status scores from wearables outperform physician estimates in predicting survival, providing a competitive advantage over current RPM solutions.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "physicians are only right 40% of the time"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Validate CrisPRO’s gait‑based performance metrics against ECOG/Karnofsky in a prospective cohort.

cited_competitors.name: Basch study (ASCO 2016)
cited_competitors.context: cited as seminal remote PRO trial showing survival benefit

cited_competitors.name: PROTECT trial
cited_competitors.context: follow‑up trial that failed to replicate survival benefit

unresolved_questions: What is the minimal set of wearable features needed to reliably predict immune‑related adverse events in checkpoint inhibitor therapy?
How can reimbursement models be aligned to support routine RPM in oncology without increasing health‑system expenses?

assets_to_track: Remote patient monitoring platforms in oncology
